title: Can I play Spotify in my store? — Daniel Fox
meta_description: No. Spotify's terms limit accounts to personal, non-commercial use, and its support page says so plainly. What being legal actually costs a store, from the primary sources.

schema.headline: Can I play Spotify in my store?
schema.description: Spotify's terms limit consumer accounts to personal, non-commercial use, and the support page prohibits business playback outright. The two separate exposures a consumer account creates in a store, and what the legal routes actually cost, sourced from the statutes and rate schedules.

faq.q: Can I play Spotify in my store if I have a Premium account?
faq.a: No. Premium changes the ads and the audio quality, and the terms stay the same: personal, non-commercial use. Spotify's support page says directly that you can't play Spotify from a business and points commercial users to Soundtrack, a licensed business service.

faq.q: What happens if a store plays Spotify anyway?
faq.a: Two separate exposures. Copyright law lets courts award $750 to $30,000 per song performed without a license, up to $150,000 per song for willful infringement. And business playback breaks Spotify's terms of use, so the account itself can be revoked. A PRO license addresses the first exposure and leaves the second in place.

faq.q: What is the cheapest legal way to play music in a store?
faq.a: For most small stores, a business streaming service. Pandora CloudCover is $18.95 a month per location, SiriusXM Music for Business is $26.95, Soundtrack starts at $29 per zone, and each bundles the performance licensing. A store under 2,000 square feet can also play FCC-licensed broadcast radio over a small speaker setup with no license at all, though that covers radio only.
